title: Nontyphoidal Salmonella: osteomyelitis in immunocompromise
vignette: A 64-year-old man comes to the office due to 3 days of diarrhea. He began to have nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramps, and diarrhea 24 hours after eating chicken salad at a fast-food restaurant. The patient has had 4-6 episodes of diarrhea per day with no blood in the stool. He has no tenesmus. His wife, who ate the same food, had a few loose stools but felt well afterward. The patient takes a tumor necrosis factor inhibitor for psoriatic arthritis and a proton pump inhibitor for gastritis. Temperature is 38 C (100 F), blood pressure is 120/70 mm Hg, and pulse is 88/min. Abdominal examination reveals mild diffuse tenderness to palpation and increased bowel sounds. Cultures of the stool yield gram-negative bacilli that are nonlactose fermenting and oxidase negative. Which of the following is the most likely complication associated with this patient's current infection?

clues.text: eating chicken salad at a fast-food restaurant
clues.note: Contaminated poultry and eggs are the classic source of nontyphoidal Salmonella (chicken salad). Campylobacter is also poultry-associated, so the source alone doesn't clinch it - the culture does.
Non-bloody, watery diarrhea fits nontyphoidal Salmonella and argues against the bloody dysentery of Shigella and Shiga toxin-producing E. coli - the two HUS-causing organisms.

Absence of tenesmus (painful urge to pass stool) argues against Shigella, which typically causes fever, tenesmus, and bloody/mucoid stool. This pushes away from the HUS and seizure (Shigella) answers.

clues.text: tumor necrosis factor inhibitor for psoriatic arthritis
clues.note: TNF-alpha inhibitors cripple macrophage killing of intracellular organisms, the key defense against Salmonella. Impaired immunity is the major risk factor for INVASIVE nontyphoidal Salmonella - bacteremia that seeds bone (osteomyelitis), vessels, or heart valves.

clues.text: a proton pump inhibitor for gastritis
clues.note: Salmonella must survive gastric acid to infect. Acid suppression (PPI) lowers the infectious dose and raises the risk and severity of Salmonella infection.

clues.text: gram-negative bacilli that are nonlactose fermenting and oxidase negative
clues.note: Nonlactose-fermenting, oxidase-NEGATIVE gram-negative rods = Salmonella or Shigella (Campylobacter is oxidase-POSITIVE and curved). Combined with non-bloody diarrhea from poultry in an immunocompromised host, this is nontyphoidal Salmonella.

whatMatters: Pin the organism, then the host. Nonlactose-fermenting, oxidase-negative GNR + non-bloody diarrhea from poultry = nontyphoidal Salmonella (not Campylobacter, which is oxidase-positive). The TNF-inhibitor + PPI mark an immunocompromised, acid-suppressed host prone to INVASIVE disease. Invasive nontyphoidal Salmonella seeds bone -> osteomyelitis (also mycotic aneurysm, endocarditis). Answer E.
strategy: Two-step infectious-disease question: (1) ID the bug from culture + stool character, (2) match the COMPLICATION to that bug and host. Salmonella -> osteomyelitis / endovascular infection (worst in sickle cell or immunosuppression). HUS and seizure belong to Shigella/EHEC (bloody diarrhea + tenesmus). Guillain-Barre follows Campylobacter (oxidase-positive, bloody). Chronic malabsorption follows Giardia.

eliminations.label: Chronic malabsorption - Giardia, not Salmonella
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: villous blunting from chronic Giardia infection causes fat malabsorption and foul, watery diarrhea. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: Salmonella gastroenteritis is self-limited and does not cause chronic malabsorption. ✅ Correct if: prolonged greasy diarrhea after camping / contaminated water.

eliminations.label: Generalized seizure - Shigella, wrong organism
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: Shigella can cause neurotoxicity (including seizures, especially in children) and is itself a nonlactose-fermenting, oxidase-negative GNR. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: Shigella causes a bloody, febrile dysentery WITH tenesmus - this patient has non-bloody stool and no tenesmus, favoring Salmonella. ✅ Correct if: bloody diarrhea with tenesmus and seizures in a young child.

eliminations.label: Guillain-Barre syndrome - Campylobacter, not this bug
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: Guillain-Barre (ascending paralysis) is classically triggered by Campylobacter jejuni, a poultry-associated enteric pathogen. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: C. jejuni is OXIDASE-POSITIVE and causes bloody diarrhea; this culture is oxidase-negative and the stool is non-bloody -> Salmonella. ✅ Correct if: ascending weakness/areflexia weeks after a diarrheal illness.

eliminations.label: Hemolytic uremic syndrome - Shigella/EHEC (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: HUS (microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, AKI) follows Shiga toxin from EHEC (O157:H7) or Shigella dysenteriae. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: those cause BLOODY diarrhea (and EHEC is lactose-FERMENTING); this is a non-bloody, nonlactose-fermenting, oxidase-negative Salmonella. The trap is matching a nonlactose-fermenting GNR to Shigella while ignoring the non-bloody, no-tenesmus stool. ✅ Correct if: bloody diarrhea after undercooked beef, then oliguria and anemia.

reasoning: Nonlactose-fermenting, oxidase-negative GNR + non-bloody diarrhea from poultry = nontyphoidal Salmonella (Campylobacter is oxidase-positive)
Host is immunosuppressed (TNF-alpha inhibitor) and acid-suppressed (PPI) -> prone to invasive disease
Invasive nontyphoidal Salmonella spreads hematogenously and seeds bone
-> osteomyelitis (also mycotic aneurysm, endocarditis); answer E
mechanism: Nontyphoidal Salmonella is acquired from contaminated poultry, eggs, or reptiles and usually causes self-limited gastroenteritis. Gastric acid is a key barrier, so acid suppression (PPI) lowers the infectious dose. The organism invades enterocytes and is taken up by macrophages; control of this intracellular infection depends on TNF-alpha-driven macrophage activation, so TNF-alpha inhibitors (and other impaired immunity, plus extremes of age) predispose to INVASIVE disease. Bacteremia can seed the vasculature (mycotic aneurysm), heart valves (endocarditis), and bone, producing osteomyelitis - classically also in sickle cell disease. Shigella and Shiga toxin-producing E. coli (HUS, seizures) cause bloody diarrhea with tenesmus, and Campylobacter (Guillain-Barre) is oxidase-positive, which distinguishes them here.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Micro > Salmonella vs Shigella vs Campylobacter (lactose, oxidase, bloody vs non-bloody); invasive nontyphoidal Salmonella -> osteomyelitis (sickle cell, immunocompromise); post-diarrheal complications (HUS = EHEC/Shigella, GBS = Campylobacter).

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Two steps: identify the organism from culture + stool, THEN pick the complication that matches that organism and an immunocompromised host. The thing being asked is the COMPLICATION, not the diagnosis.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on nonlactose-fermenting, the oxidase test, tenesmus, mycotic aneurysm, nontyphoidal Salmonella, osteomyelitis, or HUS? Tell me which.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: HUS (D) lures you because a nonlactose-fermenting GNR could be Shigella - but the non-bloody stool, absent tenesmus, and poultry source say Salmonella, whose invasive complication is osteomyelitis.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank: Salmonella + immunocompromise/sickle cell -> osteomyelitis & endovascular infection; bloody diarrhea + HUS/seizure -> Shigella/EHEC; GBS -> Campylobacter (oxidase +).

keywords.front: Nonlactose-fermenting, oxidase-negative GNR, non-bloody diarrhea from poultry

keywords.back: Extremes of age, impaired immunity (AIDS, TNF-alpha inhibitor), acid suppression (PPI)

firstAid.detail: Micro > poultry/egg source; acid-labile (PPI raises risk); invasive disease -> osteomyelitis (esp sickle cell), mycotic aneurysm, endocarditis.
firstAid.location: Micro > poultry/egg source; acid-labile (PPI raises risk); invasive disease -> osteomyelitis (esp sickle cell), mycotic aneurysm, endocarditis.

firstAid.detail: Micro > HUS (EHEC/Shigella), seizure (Shigella), Guillain-Barre (Campylobacter), chronic malabsorption (Giardia).
firstAid.location: Micro > HUS (EHEC/Shigella), seizure (Shigella), Guillain-Barre (Campylobacter), chronic malabsorption (Giardia).

firstAid.detail: Micro > nonlactose fermenters (Salmonella, Shigella); Campylobacter oxidase-positive & curved; EHEC lactose-positive.
firstAid.location: Micro > nonlactose fermenters (Salmonella, Shigella); Campylobacter oxidase-positive & curved; EHEC lactose-positive.

memoryHook: Salmon swim to the bone: Salmonella + sickle cell/immunosuppression = osteomyelitis. Bloody stool + HUS = Shigella/EHEC; Campylobacter (oxidase +) = Guillain-Barre.
